messages.content: please look at the files in our sources. we are going to use all of these concepts for our new development process called Grove

I looked through the source files that were loaded, and the strongest reusable material for Grove is the WISC/context-engineering set from the transcript and README. The core idea is that context is the scarce resource, and Grove should be designed around preventing “context rot,” not just around giving agents bigger windows. The transcript explicitly frames poor context management as the main cause of agent mistakes, especially on larger, messier codebases. 

cole_transcript

What I’d carry into Grove:

1. Grove should be context-first, not chat-first.
The README defines WISC as Write, Isolate, Select, Compress, with the ordering mattering: Write and Isolate do most of the heavy lifting, Select amplifies them, and Compress is the fallback. That is a very strong backbone for Grove. 

README

2. Grove should use a layered context system.
The README’s 3-tier model is probably the cleanest direct transplant:

a lean always-loaded global rules file

path- or domain-scoped on-demand rules

heavy reference docs that are not auto-loaded and are only pulled in when a scout/research agent finds them relevant.

3. Grove should split planning from implementation.
The README and transcript both push a fresh-session workflow where planning produces a structured spec/plan file, and implementation starts in a clean context using that plan as the main input. That is exactly the kind of discipline Grove should enforce. 

README

4. Grove should externalize memory aggressively.
The transcript emphasizes using enriched git commits as long-term memory, plus handoff/progress files and decision logs so knowledge survives context resets. That fits Grove extremely well.

5. Grove should use sub-agents mainly for research and scouting, not coding in the middle of a messy session.
The transcript is very clear here: isolate research into sub-agents, keep the main context clean, and use a scout pattern to decide which docs are worth loading.

6. Grove should support targeted priming.
The README’s /prime-* concept is important: don’t “understand the whole repo” every time; prime only the subsystem needed for the current task. 

README

So my recommendation is that Grove should not just “borrow concepts” from these files. It should be built around them.

A good Grove v1 shape would be:

GROVE.md — global rules, principles, architecture, commands

grove/rules/ — scoped rule packs by domain or path

grove/docs/ — heavy reference material, never auto-loaded

grove/plans/ — structured implementation plans/specs

grove/handoffs/ — session summaries and next actions

grove/decisions/ — ADR-style decision records

grove/commands/ — prime, plan, execute, handoff, commit

grove/skills/ — optional capability packs loaded on demand

If you want the cleanest Grove translation of WISC, I’d rename it like this:

Write → Record

Isolate → Delegate

Select → Prime

Compress → Handoff

That gives Grove its own identity while preserving the mechanics.

My blunt take: the real value here is not the acronym. It is the operating model:

plans are files

memory is externalized

context is layered

research is delegated

implementation happens in fresh sessions

long sessions end in handoff, not chaos

That is the part Grove should inherit.

I also saw the uploaded source files include README.md and cole_transcript.md, which are the ones that surfaced usable text directly in search.

Next, I can turn this into a concrete Grove development process spec with folder structure, file templates, command names, and the operating rules for plan → execute → handoff.
great job on that part. now i want you to unzip the .ai.zip file and look at what my old system looked like. it was growing out of control

Yep — I unzipped it and looked through the old .ai system.

My blunt read: it had a lot of good instincts, but it was absolutely on the path to becoming process-heavy and self-expanding.

What the old system looked like

It was organized into these major pieces:

COMMANDS/ — 21 command docs

PLANS/ — numbered implementation plans

SPECS/ — larger feature/spec docs

REF/ — supporting reference docs

RULES.md — central operating rules

HUMAN_RUNBOOK.md — prescribed command cadence

That means the system already had several overlapping layers:

global rules

human operating instructions

command-level instructions

plans

specs

reference docs

That is not inherently bad, but it creates a serious “process stack” problem. Every new feature can end up spawning:

a spec

a plan

command changes

rules changes

ref updates

handoff/status material

That is how these systems start to balloon.

What was strong about it

There were some very solid ideas in there:

No plan, no code

Execute from the plan

Validate every meaningful change

Keep changes small and reversible

Use explicit quality gates

End sessions with handoff/status discipline

That part is good. It shows real maturity.

Why it was growing out of control

Here’s the real issue: the old system was trying to govern too many layers of behavior at once.

1. Too many command surfaces

You had commands for:

prime

create-prd

create-rules

plan

execute

handoff

validate

review

status-sync

retro

tech-debt

release-notes

commit

push

pr

phase-intent-check

check_tests

library-skill

That is already a lot to remember and maintain.

The problem is not just the number. It is that several of them are operationally adjacent and partially overlapping. For example:

validate

check_tests

review

status-sync

handoff

Those are all reasonable, but together they can create a ritual burden where the process starts serving itself.

2. Plans and specs both existed as first-class systems

You had both:

.ai/PLANS/...

.ai/SPECS/...

That usually becomes dangerous unless the distinction is brutally enforced.

Because then a feature can have:

PRD

architecture doc

implementation roadmap

implementation plan

That is useful for a giant effort, but lethal when it becomes the default for ordinary work.

3. The rules had started accreting

RULES.md itself was actually pretty decent, but it was already becoming a “policy gravity well.”

It included:

workflow invariants

quality gates

architectural boundaries

typing guidance

security guidance

documentation hygiene

project-type references

output conventions

Lily-specific rules

That is the classic pattern:
start with a clean rule file, then every pain point adds another section, then the rulebook becomes another thing agents must load, interpret, and reconcile.

4. The system was mixing global process with project-specific policy

The old system was not just a generic AI-dev process. It was also carrying Lily-specific conventions.

That means the framework and the project were starting to fuse together.

That is one of the biggest reasons these systems become unwieldy:

you think you are building a reusable operating model

but you are actually embedding one project’s scars into the framework itself

5. It was drifting toward ceremony

The HUMAN_RUNBOOK.md showed a pretty strict cadence:

prime

maybe create PRD

plan

branch setup

phase intent check

execute

status sync

validate

review

commit

push

pr

handoff

That is disciplined, but for day-to-day development it is also a lot.

When a system requires that many explicit steps, one of two things happens:

people stop following it consistently

or they spend too much energy feeding the system

Either way, it stops being a sharp tool.

My diagnosis

The old .ai system had too many nouns and too many required artifacts.

It was evolving into a meta-process framework rather than a lean development system.

In other words:
it wasn’t just helping you build software anymore — it was starting to become software you had to manage.

That’s the warning sign.

What Grove should keep

Grove should preserve the strongest bones:

planning before coding

isolated context

explicit handoffs

lean rules

clear validation gates

externalized decisions

What Grove should cut hard

Grove should be much more ruthless about limiting surface area.

I would strongly recommend Grove v1 have only these core concepts:

Rules — one lean global rule file

Plans — one implementation plan per meaningful feature

Prime — targeted context loading

Execute — do the work from the plan

Validate — prove it works

Handoff — leave clean state for the next session

That’s it.

Not:

rules + runbook + specs + plans + command zoo + multiple report types

Instead:

one compact operating spine

My recommendation for Grove

I would structure Grove around 5 folders max:

grove/GROVE.md

grove/plans/

grove/rules/ or just scoped rule files if truly needed

grove/ref/

grove/handoffs/

And command-wise, I’d keep Grove to maybe:

prime

plan

execute

validate

handoff

Then maybe later:

review

retro

But only if they prove necessary.

Bottom line

Your old system was not bad. It was actually thoughtful and fairly advanced.

But yes — it was growing out of control because it was accumulating:

too many commands

too many artifact types

too many layers of instruction

too much ceremony

too much project-specific policy in the framework

That gives us a very clean design rule for Grove:

Grove should be opinionated, but smaller.
Structured, but not ritualistic.
Layered, but not sprawling.

The old .ai system is useful as a cautionary fossil.

Next I can turn this into a “What Grove keeps / What Grove kills / What Grove renames” blueprint.
now unzip this and we can use it as inspiration of the direction to take

I unpacked the ai-coding-wisc-framework.zip and compared it to your old .ai system. This one is actually much closer to the direction Grove should take. It’s far more disciplined about context management and artifact boundaries, which is exactly the thing that was starting to break in your previous setup.

Let me walk through the key pieces and what they imply for Grove.

What this WISC framework is doing right

The framework is built around one core philosophy:

Context is the scarce resource.

Everything in the repo supports that idea.

Instead of building a big command system, it builds a context architecture.

The structure looked roughly like:

/rules
/docs
/commands
/skills

But the important part is how those are used, not the folders themselves.

The 4 pillars (WISC)

The README defines the operating model:

W — Write
Document decisions, plans, architecture.

I — Isolate
Separate concerns so the agent only loads relevant information.

S — Select
Load only the context needed for the task.

C — Compress
Summarize or offload context to maintain clarity.

This is essentially context engineering for development workflows.

Key architectural patterns worth stealing for Grove
1. Lean global rule file

There is a very small always-loaded rule set.

Example idea:

RULES.md

Contains things like:

architecture boundaries

code quality expectations

workflow expectations

repo structure rules

But critically:

It stays short.

Heavy documentation is pushed elsewhere.

This prevents the rule file from becoming the giant gravity well your .ai system was drifting toward.

2. Scoped rule loading

Rules can be loaded based on directory or domain.

Example concept:

rules/
    backend.md
    frontend.md
    infra.md

Or even path-based:

rules/src-backend.md
rules/src-frontend.md

So the agent loads rules only when touching those areas.

That is extremely important for large projects.

3. Heavy docs live in a separate reference layer

Instead of bloating context, large docs are stored here:

docs/

Examples:

architecture docs

integration guides

library documentation

deep system explanations

These are never automatically loaded.

Instead:

Agents or commands can retrieve them on demand.

This is the scout pattern mentioned in the transcript.

4. Command structure is intentionally minimal

Unlike your .ai system, this one avoids a command explosion.

Commands focus on context preparation, not ritual.

Examples include things like:

prime
plan
execute

Rather than:

prime
create-prd
create-rules
phase-intent-check
check_tests
status-sync
retro
release-notes
...

The difference is huge.

This system avoids turning the framework into a bureaucracy generator.

5. Planning happens outside the execution context

This is a big one.

The framework pushes this workflow:

Session 1 — planning

prime → research → plan

Produce a plan document.

Then end the session.

Session 2 — execution

New clean context.

Load:

plan + rules + relevant files

Then implement.

This prevents the common AI failure mode:

messy brainstorming context contaminates coding context.

6. Sub-agents are used for research

Instead of having the main context balloon with exploration, the system uses:

scout agents

They:

search docs

gather context

summarize findings

Then return a compressed summary.

The main agent only loads the result.

This is very powerful.

Where your old .ai system differed

Your system was evolving into:

framework = workflow engine

This WISC framework is more like:

framework = context architecture

That difference matters a lot.

One governs process steps.

The other governs information flow.

The latter scales better.

What Grove should inherit from WISC

I would strongly recommend Grove adopt these ideas almost directly:

1. Context layering
GROVE.md         ← always loaded rules
rules/           ← scoped rule files
docs/            ← heavy references
plans/           ← implementation plans
handoffs/        ← session summaries
2. Planning vs execution separation

Never mix:

research
planning
implementation

in the same context.

That’s one of the biggest improvements you can make.

3. Scout pattern

Use sub-agents to:

search docs

retrieve context

summarize

Return compressed information to the main agent.

4. Minimal command surface

Instead of 20 commands, Grove should probably have:

prime
plan
execute
validate
handoff

That’s enough.

What Grove should improve beyond WISC

Because Grove is your system, I’d add two upgrades.

1. Decision records (ADR)

WISC does not formalize architectural decisions enough.

Add:

grove/decisions/

Example:

0003-use-pydantic-state.md
0004-adopt-langgraph-supervisor.md

This prevents decisions from disappearing into chat history.

2. Agent memory layer

Given your work with OpenClaw and Chrona, Grove should include:

grove/memory/

For things like:

coding-style.md
project-preferences.md
tooling.md
lessons-learned.md

That way agents retain long-term behavioral knowledge.

What Grove should NOT copy

Two things.

Do not recreate the .ai command explosion

Your old system was drifting toward:

framework = ceremony engine

Grove should stay lean.

Do not allow the rule file to grow indefinitely

If a rule file exceeds:

~200 lines

Split it.

Otherwise it becomes the same problem again.

My honest assessment

Between the three systems we’ve looked at:

Old .ai

Good ideas but turning into process sprawl.

WISC framework

Excellent context discipline.

This is the right philosophical base.

Grove (what you’re building)

Should be:

WISC-style context architecture
+
your agent ecosystem
+
lean artifact structure

That combination will scale much better than the old system.

If you want, the next thing I can do is something extremely useful:

I can design Grove v1 as a complete system including:

exact folder structure

rule templates

plan templates

handoff templates

command definitions

agent interaction model

Basically the first production-ready version of Grove.

here is what i really want to do. i want to make a cli that generates perfect skills for a project. we will have all the pieces you mentioned, but we could have a little tui that lets us select what pieces we want and then takes templates and writes all the pieces into the correct spot. make sense?

Yes. It makes very good sense.

What you’re describing is not just “a CLI for skills.” It’s really a skill scaffolder + project context installer.

That is a strong direction because it solves the exact problem we just uncovered:

Your system should not depend on manually curating a sprawling .ai tree every time. Instead, Grove should generate a lean, opinionated structure on demand from templates.

The big idea

You build a CLI/TUI that does three things:

Understands the project type

Python library

web app

agent system

research project

monorepo

CLI tool

media pipeline

etc.

Lets the user select capabilities

core rules

planning files

handoff flow

decisions/ADRs

scoped rules

memory/preferences

validation recipes

command prompts

skill packs

Materializes the selected system into the repo

writes files into the right places

fills templates

wires references

avoids dumping unnecessary ceremony

That gives you a tailored Grove install instead of a one-size-fits-all process blob.

The key mental model

Think of Grove as having three layers:

1. Grove Core

The minimal universal pieces:

GROVE.md

plans/

handoffs/

maybe decisions/

2. Grove Packs

Optional modules:

Python strict typing pack

LangGraph agent pack

research writing pack

monorepo pack

API/backend pack

frontend/UI pack

Docker/infra pack

3. Project Skills

Custom skills generated for that specific repo:

“how to work in this codebase”

“how to plan changes here”

“how to validate changes here”

“what architectural boundaries matter here”

That last layer is probably the real magic.

The CLI/TUI should feel like this

Something like:

Bash
grove init

Then a TUI wizard:

Project name: nova-architect

Project type: agent framework

Language: python

Interface: cli

Repo shape: single repo

Add planning system? yes

Add ADRs? yes

Add scoped rules? yes

Add validation recipes? yes

Add memory/preferences? yes

Add LangGraph pack? yes

Add Docker pack? no

Add research notes pack? no

Then it generates:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  plans/
  handoffs/
  decisions/
  rules/
  memory/
  skills/
  templates/

And optionally:

starter files

starter commands

project-specific prompt templates

rule packs for Python/LangGraph/etc.

I would separate “skills” into two kinds

This part matters a lot.

A. Static skill templates

These are reusable blueprints shipped with Grove.

Examples:

python-library-skill

langgraph-agent-skill

strict-typing-skill

research-project-skill

These are basically parameterized templates.

B. Generated project skills

These are created for the actual repository after analyzing it.

Examples:

working-with-state-models.md

editing-cli-commands.md

testing-patterns.md

repo-architecture.md

So the flow becomes:

install Grove core

choose packs

scan the repo

generate project-specific skills from templates + repo facts

That is much more powerful than just copying files.

Best architecture for the tool

I’d structure the tool around these components.

1. Template Registry

Knows what templates exist.

Example concepts:

core/grove-base

packs/python

packs/langgraph

packs/research

skills/project-architecture

skills/testing-pattern

2. Project Analyzer

Inspects the repo and infers:

language

frameworks

package manager

test framework

repo layout

common directories

config files

CI tooling

This can power smart defaults.

3. Install Planner

Builds an installation plan before writing files.

For example:

create .grove/GROVE.md

create .grove/plans/README.md

create .grove/rules/python.md

skip frontend rules

generate skill_testing.md from pytest template

4. Renderer

Renders templates with variables like:

project name

language

framework

paths

coding conventions

package manager

test commands

5. File Writer

Writes files safely:

preview mode

diff mode

merge mode

overwrite protection

6. TUI Front End

Lets you:

pick packs

preview output

toggle components

regenerate pieces later

The TUI should not just be for init

It should also support ongoing management.

For example:

Bash
grove manage

And then let you:

add a new pack

regenerate a skill

update templates

remove unused pieces

sync Grove files with current repo structure

That keeps Grove alive without turning it into manual maintenance hell.

The killer feature

The smartest version of this tool would have:

Bash
grove analyze

It scans the repo and says something like:

Detected Python project with uv

Detected Typer CLI

Detected pytest

Detected mypy + ruff

Detected LangGraph usage

Detected Docker

Suggested packs:

Python Core

CLI Development

Typed Architecture

LangGraph Agents

Validation Workflow

Then you accept or tweak.

That would be excellent.

What “perfect skills” probably means in practice

Not literally perfect.

It means each skill should be:

scoped to a real task

grounded in the repo’s actual structure

short enough to load

explicit about when to use it

paired with concrete file paths and commands

composable with other skills

So a skill should not be a giant philosophy doc.

It should be more like:

purpose

when to use

inputs needed

files to inspect

steps

validation

outputs

That keeps them operational.

Strong recommendation on file layout

I would keep generated Grove content inside one root:

.grove/

Then something like:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  rules/
  plans/
  handoffs/
  decisions/
  memory/
  skills/
  templates/
  manifest.toml

The manifest.toml is important. It tracks:

installed packs

generated files

template versions

project metadata

Without that, your installer becomes hard to update cleanly.

What the CLI commands could be

A very clean v1 command set:

Bash
grove init
grove analyze
grove add
grove generate-skill
grove sync
grove doctor
grove manage

What they do:

init — start a Grove setup

analyze — inspect repo and recommend packs

add — add a pack or component

generate-skill — create a new skill from templates + repo facts

sync — update generated files when repo evolves

doctor — detect broken references, stale files, missing pieces

manage — TUI dashboard

My blunt take

This is a much better idea than manually authoring every Grove file by hand.

Because then Grove becomes:

reproducible

configurable

small by default

expandable on purpose

easier to keep clean

That is exactly how you avoid repeating the .ai sprawl problem.

The one trap to avoid

Do not make the generator itself produce everything by default.

That would recreate the same problem in a new form.

Instead:

start tiny

let users opt into packs

generate only what is needed

support later expansion

Grove should be assembled, not dumped.

The direction I’d recommend

Build Grove as:

a template-driven CLI/TUI that installs a minimal context-engineering system into a repo, then generates project-specific skills from reusable packs plus repo analysis

That’s the product.

The next thing to design is the actual system spec:

folder structure

manifest schema

pack model

skill template schema

CLI commands

TUI screens

generation flow

I can draft that spec next.
im guessing we would have a base pack that is required?

Yes — and you absolutely should have a required Base Pack.

In fact, Grove will be much more stable if it treats packs the same way a package manager treats dependencies:

Base pack (required)

Optional packs (extensions)

Think of the base pack as the kernel of the Grove system.

Everything else plugs into it.

Grove Pack Model
1. Base Pack (Required)

Every Grove project installs this automatically.

Example:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  manifest.toml
  rules/
  plans/
  handoffs/
  decisions/
  skills/

This pack defines the core operating system for development.

What Base Pack Contains
1️⃣ Core rules
.grove/GROVE.md

Defines:

philosophy

WISC model

workflow

context rules

planning requirements

handoff discipline

This is the only always-loaded context file.

Everything else is selective.

2️⃣ Plan system
.grove/plans/

Includes:

PLAN_TEMPLATE.md

Defines how plans are written.

Typical sections:

Goal
Scope
Constraints
Architecture
Implementation Steps
Validation
Risks
3️⃣ Handoff system
.grove/handoffs/

Template example:

HANDOFF_TEMPLATE.md

Captures:

session summary

next tasks

open questions

decisions made

This solves the context reset problem.

4️⃣ Decision records (ADR)
.grove/decisions/

Template:

ADR_TEMPLATE.md

Example:

Decision
Context
Options
Chosen Approach
Consequences

Without this, architectural decisions disappear into chat history.

5️⃣ Skill template system
.grove/skills/

Base pack provides the skill schema.

Example:

SKILL_TEMPLATE.md

Sections like:

Purpose
When to use
Inputs
Files to inspect
Steps
Validation
Output

This enforces consistent skill design.

6️⃣ Manifest file
TOML
.grove/manifest.toml

Tracks:

project_name
grove_version
installed_packs
generated_skills
language
framework
last_sync

Without this, the CLI cannot safely manage the project.

Optional Packs

Everything else becomes an optional extension pack.

Examples:

Python Pack

Adds:

.grove/rules/python.md
.grove/skills/python-testing.md
.grove/skills/python-refactoring.md
.grove/skills/python-packaging.md
LangGraph Pack

Adds:

.grove/rules/langgraph.md
.grove/skills/agent-design.md
.grove/skills/graph-node-creation.md
Research Pack

Adds:

.grove/skills/literature-review.md
.grove/skills/experiment-design.md
.grove/skills/paper-writing.md
CLI Project Pack

Adds:

.grove/skills/cli-command-design.md
.grove/skills/cli-testing.md
Monorepo Pack

Adds:

.grove/rules/monorepo.md
.grove/skills/subproject-management.md
Example Grove Installation

User runs:

grove init

TUI:

Project type: Agent Framework
Language: Python
Detected: uv + Typer + LangGraph

Suggested packs:

[x] Base Pack (required)
[x] Python Pack
[x] LangGraph Pack
[ ] Research Pack
[ ] Web Pack

CLI installs:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  manifest.toml

  rules/
    python.md
    langgraph.md

  plans/
  handoffs/
  decisions/

  skills/
    python-testing.md
    agent-design.md
Important Design Principle

Base pack must remain extremely small.

If the base pack becomes large, you recreate the .ai problem.

Target size:

~6–10 files

That’s it.

Grove Pack Hierarchy

Think of it like this:

Grove CLI
   │
   └── Base Pack (required)
          │
          ├── Core rules
          ├── Plans
          ├── Handoffs
          ├── Decisions
          └── Skill schema
                 │
                 └── Optional Packs
                        ├── Python
                        ├── LangGraph
                        ├── Research
                        ├── CLI
                        └── etc

This is exactly how systems like:

Cargo

npm

Homebrew

Terraform modules

remain manageable.

The Real Power Move

Later you can support community packs.

Example:

grove pack install fastapi
grove pack install rllib
grove pack install kubernetes

Each pack adds:

rules

skills

templates

Suddenly Grove becomes an ecosystem.

My Honest Opinion

This CLI idea is the correct direction.

Without it:

You end up manually maintaining dozens of context files.

With it:

You get:

reproducible project setup

modular process

lean repos

automatic skill generation

And most importantly:

You avoid repeating the .ai explosion problem.

If you'd like, I can also show you something extremely useful next:

The ideal Grove CLI architecture

Including:

Python package layout

Typer CLI

template engine

pack registry

TUI implementation

project analyzer

Basically the engineering blueprint for building Grove itself.
yes, give me tons of details about each of these. make sure to have the tui flow for setup and how the setup is dynamic without having to hard code everything

Yes. Here’s the full shape I’d recommend for Grove.

The central principle is this:

Grove is not a pile of files.
It is a template-driven system installer with a dynamic registry, a repo analyzer, and a composition engine that decides what to generate.

That is how you avoid hard-coding every possible workflow.

1. What Grove actually is

At the highest level, Grove should be a tool that can:

inspect a repo

infer what kind of project it is

ask the user what operating pieces they want

install a minimal core

compose optional packs

generate project-specific skills from templates

keep everything tracked in a manifest

later update, regenerate, or remove pieces safely

So the product is really:

Grove = scaffold engine + pack system + skill generator + lifecycle manager

Not just a CLI.
Not just a TUI.
Not just templates.

It is a structured system for installing and managing development process artifacts.

2. Core architectural layers

I would split Grove into these major layers:

A. CLI layer

The command-line entry point.

Responsibilities:

parse commands

call the application services

support non-interactive use

support scripting and CI integration

Examples:

grove init

grove analyze

grove add

grove remove

grove sync

grove manage

grove doctor

grove generate-skill

The CLI should be the automation-friendly interface.

B. TUI layer

The interactive setup and management interface.

Responsibilities:

guide users through setup

show detected repo metadata

let users choose packs/components

preview files to be created

show diffs

manage installed packs over time

The TUI should be the ergonomic interface.

C. Registry layer

This is where the dynamic behavior comes from.

Responsibilities:

discover base pack, packs, templates, and generators

expose metadata about them

resolve dependencies

validate compatibility

avoid hard-coded menus

This is one of the most important pieces.

You do not want the TUI to have things like:

if python then show Python Pack

if langgraph then show LangGraph Pack

Instead, the TUI should ask the registry:

what packs exist?

what do they require?

what signals activate them?

what questions do they contribute to setup?

what files do they generate?

That makes the system extensible.

D. Analyzer layer

The analyzer inspects the repository and produces structured facts.

Responsibilities:

detect language(s)

detect frameworks

detect tools

detect project layout

detect package manager

detect test/lint/type-check tools

detect whether the repo already has Grove installed

suggest packs

The analyzer should never directly write files.
It only returns structured project metadata and recommendations.

E. Composition layer

This is the planner that decides what to install.

Responsibilities:

combine user selections, analyzer output, and pack rules

resolve dependencies

choose templates

compute variables

generate an installation plan

identify file collisions

identify upgrades vs new writes

This is the brain of setup.

F. Template/rendering layer

This renders files from templates plus variables.

Responsibilities:

fill placeholders

apply conditionals

apply loops

resolve template inheritance

generate final content

optionally generate structured docs from schema

This should support more than dumb string replacement.

G. File operation layer

This safely applies changes to disk.

Responsibilities:

preview writes

create directories

write files

merge where appropriate

detect collisions

backup or diff on overwrite

support dry-run mode

This should be careful and boring. That is a compliment.

H. Lifecycle/state layer

Tracks what Grove has installed and why.

Responsibilities:

maintain manifest

track installed packs

record generated files

track generator versions

track variables used

support regeneration and cleanup

Without this, Grove becomes a one-shot scaffold tool. You want more than that.

3. Recommended Python package structure

Since you like disciplined structure, I’d do something like this:

src/grove/
├── cli/
│   ├── app.py
│   ├── commands/
│   │   ├── init.py
│   │   ├── analyze.py
│   │   ├── add.py
│   │   ├── remove.py
│   │   ├── sync.py
│   │   ├── doctor.py
│   │   ├── manage.py
│   │   └── generate_skill.py
│
├── tui/
│   ├── app.py
│   ├── screens/
│   │   ├── welcome.py
│   │   ├── analyze.py
│   │   ├── pack_selection.py
│   │   ├── component_selection.py
│   │   ├── preview.py
│   │   ├── conflicts.py
│   │   └── finish.py
│   ├── widgets/
│   └── state.py
│
├── core/
│   ├── models.py
│   ├── enums.py
│   ├── manifest.py
│   ├── installer.py
│   ├── composer.py
│   ├── renderer.py
│   ├── file_ops.py
│   └── registry.py
│
├── analyzer/
│   ├── engine.py
│   ├── detectors/
│   │   ├── python.py
│   │   ├── javascript.py
│   │   ├── langgraph.py
│   │   ├── typer.py
│   │   ├── uv.py
│   │   ├── pytest.py
│   │   ├── ruff.py
│   │   └── mypy.py
│   └── models.py
│
├── packs/
│   ├── loader.py
│   ├── schemas.py
│   └── builtins/
│       ├── base/
│       ├── python/
│       ├── cli/
│       ├── langgraph/
│       ├── monorepo/
│       └── research/
│
├── templates/
│   ├── engine.py
│   ├── filters.py
│   └── builtins/
│
├── skills/
│   ├── generator.py
│   ├── schemas.py
│   └── inference.py
│
├── doctor/
│   ├── checks.py
│   └── reports.py
│
└── utils/
    ├── paths.py
    ├── yaml.py
    ├── toml.py
    └── logging.py

This keeps responsibilities separate and makes the tool composable.

4. Dynamic registry design

This is the part that keeps setup from being hard-coded.

The problem

Most scaffold tools cheat.
They hard-code:

pack names

prompts

dependencies

template paths

menus

That works for five things, then turns into spaghetti.

The solution

Use a registry-driven manifest model for every installable unit.

Every pack should describe itself in a manifest file.

For example:

packs/builtins/python/pack.yaml

Example shape:

YAML
id: python
name: Python Pack
version: 1.0.0
description: Rules, templates, and skills for Python projects
depends_on:
  - base
compatible_with:
  languages: [python]
activates_when:
  any:
    - file_exists: pyproject.toml
    - file_exists: requirements.txt
contributes:
  setup_questions:
    - python_package_manager
    - python_quality_tools
  templates:
    - id: python_rules
      src: templates/rules/python.md.j2
      dst: .grove/rules/python.md
    - id: python_testing_skill
      src: templates/skills/python_testing.md.j2
      dst: .grove/skills/python-testing.md
  analyzers:
    - python
    - pytest
  skills:
    - python-testing
    - python-refactoring
tags:
  - language
  - python

The registry loads all of these manifests and exposes them as structured objects.

Now the TUI does not need to know what a Python Pack is.
It just asks the registry for all compatible packs.

Registry responsibilities

The registry should answer questions like:

what packs exist?

what is required?

what depends on what?

what setup questions do active packs contribute?

what templates do selected packs generate?

what analyzers should run?

what skills are available to generate?

what conditions make a pack recommended or hidden?

This is what makes setup dynamic.

5. Pack model

I’d define three kinds of packs.

A. Base pack

Always required.
Provides:

GROVE.md

manifest

plan template

handoff template

ADR template

skill schema template

maybe a tiny core rules directory

This should be intentionally tiny.

B. Capability packs

Reusable modular packs.

Examples:

Python

CLI

LangGraph

Monorepo

Research

Docker

API Backend

Frontend

Documentation

These add:

scoped rules

skill templates

plan sections

analysis detectors

setup questions

commands metadata if needed

C. Project-local packs

These can be generated inside a repo later.

For example:

project-architecture

repo-testing-patterns

state-management

api-conventions

These are based on repo analysis and become custom skills or local packs.

That gives you both standardization and specialization.

6. How setup becomes dynamic

This is the heart of your question.

You do not want setup flow hard-coded like:

ask for project type

ask for language

ask for framework

ask if Python pack

ask if LangGraph pack

That will become brittle.

Instead, use a question contribution system.

Idea: packs contribute setup questions

Each pack can declare setup questions.

Example:

YAML
setup_questions:
  - id: python_package_manager
    type: select
    label: Python package manager
    options: [uv, poetry, pip]
    default_from_analysis: package_manager
    show_when:
      language_is: python

Another pack:

YAML
setup_questions:
  - id: add_adrs
    type: confirm
    label: Install architectural decision records?
    default: true

Another:

YAML
setup_questions:
  - id: langgraph_style
    type: select
    label: LangGraph workflow style
    options: [supervisor_tools, explicit_graph, hybrid]
    show_when:
      pack_selected: langgraph

Now the TUI builds its questions dynamically by aggregating all contributed questions from selected or recommended packs.

That is how you avoid hard coding.

Dynamic flow algorithm

A good setup flow works like this:

Step 1: bootstrap

Run only a tiny set of universal questions:

install location

project root confirmation

maybe project name if not obvious

Step 2: analyze

Scan the repo and collect structured signals.

Example output:

YAML
languages:
  - python
tools:
  package_manager: uv
  test_framework: pytest
  linter: ruff
  type_checker: mypy
frameworks:
  - typer
  - langgraph
repo_shape: single_package
has_docker: true
Step 3: registry recommendations

Registry compares pack conditions against analysis and returns:

required packs

recommended packs

optional packs

incompatible packs

Step 4: pack selection

TUI shows dynamic pack list based on registry output.

Step 5: question aggregation

For the selected packs, gather all setup questions.

Step 6: resolve conditions

Only show questions whose conditions are met.

Step 7: compute generation plan

The composer decides:

which templates to render

which variables are needed

which files will be created

which files already exist

whether files are managed, local-only, or mergeable

Step 8: preview

Show the exact plan.

Step 9: apply

Write files and update manifest.

That’s fully dynamic.

7. TUI flow for first-time setup

Here’s the flow I’d recommend.

Screen 1: Welcome

Purpose:

explain what Grove will do

detect repo root

confirm working directory

Show:

current path

repo status

whether .grove/manifest.toml already exists

Actions:

initialize

reconfigure existing install

quit

Screen 2: Repository Analysis

Purpose:

run detectors

show inferred project facts

Display:

language(s)

frameworks

package manager

testing tools

repo layout

CI tools

confidence level

Example:

Language: Python

Package manager: uv

CLI framework: Typer

Agent framework: LangGraph

Testing: pytest

Type checking: mypy

Linting: ruff

Actions:

accept analysis

override fields

rescan

Important:
This screen should let the user correct analysis without changing code.

Screen 3: Core Install

Purpose:

confirm base pack

select install root

select baseline components

Options:

install .grove/

include ADRs

include handoffs

include scoped rules directory

include memory/preferences directory

include project-specific skills directory

Some of these can be optional, but Base Pack itself is not.

Screen 4: Recommended Packs

Purpose:

show recommended packs based on analysis

Layout:

Required

Recommended

Available

Incompatible

Each pack card shows:

name

why recommended

what it adds

dependencies

estimated file count

Example:

Python Pack — recommended because pyproject.toml detected

CLI Pack — recommended because Typer detected

LangGraph Pack — recommended because langgraph imports detected

Actions:

toggle selection

view details

Screen 5: Pack Configuration

Purpose:

ask contributed setup questions for selected packs

This screen is fully dynamic.

Examples:

use uv commands in generated docs?

include strict typing guidance?

include pytest validation skill?

LangGraph style?

monorepo subproject roots?

The order should be determined by dependencies and categories, not hard-coded position.

Screen 6: Components Preview

Purpose:

show exactly what will be installed

Display:

folders to create

files to write

files skipped

files that already exist

generated skills

managed vs unmanaged files

This screen should feel like a package installer preview.

Screen 7: Conflicts

Purpose:

resolve overwrite or merge issues

For each collision:

overwrite

keep existing

rename generated file

diff preview

This is critical for trust.

Screen 8: Final Review

Purpose:

summarize install plan

Example:

Install root: .grove/

Packs: base, python, cli, langgraph

Files to create: 17

Files to update: 1

Skills to generate: 6

Action:

apply installation

Screen 9: Finish

Purpose:

confirm success

show next commands

Example:

grove doctor

grove manage

grove generate-skill

grove sync

Also show the installed pack list and manifest location.

8. TUI flow for ongoing management

You also want a management TUI, not just init.

Main dashboard sections

Installed packs

Repo analysis

Generated skills

Sync status

Drift warnings

Suggested additions

Actions:

add pack

remove pack

re-run analysis

regenerate skill

update managed files

doctor checks

preview manifest

This turns Grove from a one-time installer into a living system.

9. Manifest design

This is one of the most important files.

Example:

TOML
[grove]
version = "0.1.0"
installed_at = "2026-03-16T18:00:00Z"

[project]
name = "petal"
root = "."
repo_shape = "single_repo"

[analysis]
languages = ["python"]
frameworks = ["typer", "langgraph"]
package_manager = "uv"
test_framework = "pytest"
linter = "ruff"
type_checker = "mypy"

[packs]
installed = ["base", "python", "cli", "langgraph"]

[options]
include_adrs = true
include_handoffs = true
include_memory = true
langgraph_style = "supervisor_tools"

[[generated_files]]
path = ".grove/GROVE.md"
source = "base:grove_md"
managed = true
checksum = "..."

[[generated_files]]
path = ".grove/rules/python.md"
source = "python:python_rules"
managed = true
checksum = "..."

[[generated_skills]]
id = "python-testing"
path = ".grove/skills/python-testing.md"
source = "python:python_testing_skill"
managed = true

The manifest lets Grove:

know what it owns

know where files came from

know how to regenerate them

detect drift

remove packs safely

Without this, cleanup and sync become painful.

10. Template engine design

You want enough power to be flexible, but not so much that templates become code spaghetti.

I’d use a templating engine like Jinja2, but wrap it in your own schema.

Each template descriptor should define:

template source

destination path

required variables

optional conditions

merge strategy

whether the file is managed

Example:

YAML
id: python_rules
src: templates/rules/python.md.j2
dst: .grove/rules/python.md
managed: true
merge_strategy: replace
requires:
  - project_name
  - package_manager
  - test_framework
render_when:
  language_is: python

This keeps templates declarative.

11. Merge and file ownership strategy

Not every file should be treated the same.

Use ownership categories:

Managed files

Grove owns the whole file.
Can regenerate safely.

Examples:

.grove/rules/python.md

.grove/templates/PLAN_TEMPLATE.md

Seeded files

Grove creates them once, but expects user edits.
Do not overwrite automatically.

Examples:

project-architecture.md

repo-specific skill drafts

Anchored files

Grove inserts blocks into marked regions.

Example:

Markdown
<!-- GROVE:BEGIN -->
... generated section ...
<!-- GROVE:END -->

This is useful if you ever want to update a README or project doc without owning the full file.

This distinction matters a lot for trust and usability.

12. Repo analyzer design

The analyzer should be plugin-based too.

Each detector should:

inspect specific signals

return structured evidence

return confidence

not mutate anything

Example detectors:

PyprojectDetector

UvDetector

PytestDetector

RuffDetector

MypyDetector

TyperDetector

LangGraphDetector

DockerDetector

GitHubActionsDetector

Each produces facts like:

Python
Run
DetectedFact(
    key="package_manager",
    value="uv",
    confidence=0.98,
    evidence=["pyproject.toml contains tool.uv", "uv.lock exists"],
)

Then the analyzer engine consolidates facts into a normalized ProjectProfile.

That profile feeds pack recommendations and template variables.

13. Skill generation model

A generated skill should not just be a copied markdown file.

It should be rendered from:

pack template

analyzer profile

optional repo scan

user preferences

maybe selected paths/modules

Example generated skill:
python-testing.md

Could include:

actual test command: uv run pytest

actual type-check command: uv run mypy src

actual lint command: uv run ruff check .

repo-specific test directories

conventions based on current structure

So the skill feels native to the repo, not generic.

Skill schema

I’d use a standard structure like:

Markdown
# Skill: Python Testing

## Purpose
What this skill is for.

## Use When
Situations where this skill should be loaded.

## Repo Facts
Relevant repo-specific facts.

## Inspect First
Files/directories to inspect before changing code.

## Steps
Ordered workflow.

## Validation
Exact commands to run.

## Output Expectations
What a good result looks like.

## Related Skills
Optional adjacent skills.

This keeps them operational and bounded.

14. Pack dependency system

Packs should support dependencies and optional enhancements.

Examples:

cli depends on base

langgraph depends on python

fastapi depends on python

monorepo depends on base only

Also allow soft recommendations:

if langgraph selected, recommend cli if Typer detected

if python selected and pytest detected, recommend testing skill generation

The dependency graph should be resolved by the composer, not the TUI.

15. What the CLI commands should do

I’d make them like this.

grove init

Interactive or semi-interactive installation.
Runs setup wizard.
Can also support flags for automation.

grove analyze

Only inspect the repo and print structured recommendations.

grove add <pack>

Install an additional pack later.

grove remove <pack>

Remove a pack and optionally prune managed files.

grove sync

Re-render managed files using current manifest and template versions.

grove generate-skill

Generate one or more project-specific skills.

Example:
grove generate-skill testing
grove generate-skill architecture --path src/petal

grove manage

Launch TUI dashboard.

grove doctor

Check for:

stale manifest

missing files

drift

broken references

incompatible packs

16. How dynamic setup avoids hard-coded screens

Here’s the practical mechanism.

Each pack can contribute:

recommendation rules

setup questions

templates

analyzers

post-install hooks

doctor checks

skills

The setup engine then operates generically:

load all pack manifests

run relevant analyzers

compute recommended pack set

let user choose packs

aggregate questions from selected packs

sort questions by dependency/order metadata

collect answers

compute template variables

render install plan

preview and apply

That means the TUI doesn’t need to know:

what Python is

what LangGraph is

what research pack is

It just knows how to render pack metadata and question schemas.

That’s the right abstraction.

17. TUI implementation approach

Since you mentioned a little TUI, I’d likely use Textual.

Why:

modern Python TUI

structured widgets

good state management

nice layouts

supports lists, forms, tabs, previews

strong fit for a multi-screen installer

You can model setup state as a central object:

Python
Run
class SetupState(BaseModel):
    project_root: Path
    analysis_profile: ProjectProfile
    selected_packs: list[str]
    answers: dict[str, Any]
    install_plan: InstallPlan | None
    conflicts: list[FileConflict]

Each screen reads and updates shared state.
No hard-coded business logic in widgets.

Widgets should remain dumb. Business logic lives in services.

18. Suggested data models

At minimum, you want models like:

ProjectProfile

PackManifest

SetupQuestion

TemplateSpec

InstallPlan

PlannedFile

ManifestState

GeneratedSkill

DetectedFact

DoctorIssue

These should be Pydantic models so validation is strict.

That will save you pain later.

19. How packs can stay open-ended

You asked specifically about avoiding hard-coding everything.

The long-term answer is to let packs be distributed as data plus templates, not code only.

A pack can be mostly:

a pack.yaml

a set of templates

optional Python hooks for advanced logic

That means a new pack can often be added without changing Grove core.

For example, a future rllib pack might be:

packs/rllib/
  pack.yaml
  templates/
    rules/rllib.md.j2
    skills/experiment-runner.md.j2

And the registry just discovers it.

That is exactly what you want.

20. My blunt recommendations

Here are the design choices I think are non-negotiable if you want Grove to stay clean.

First, Base Pack must be tiny.
If Base Pack gets bloated, Grove dies the same death as .ai.

Second, registry-driven everything.
Packs, questions, templates, recommendations, and checks should be data-driven.

Third, manifest-backed lifecycle.
If Grove can’t track what it owns, it will become dangerous to use.

Fourth, analysis should inform, not dictate.
The user can always override.

Fifth, generated skills must be repo-specific.
Otherwise they become fluffy generic docs nobody uses.

Sixth, TUI must preview before writing.
Trust matters.

Seventh, support add/remove/sync from day one.
Otherwise the scaffold will drift immediately.

21. The shape of a very strong v1

If I were scoping a serious first version, I’d target this:

Base capabilities

grove init

grove analyze

grove manage

grove add

grove sync

manifest support

base pack

python pack

cli pack

langgraph pack

Textual TUI

preview/install plan

managed vs seeded files

repo-specific skill generation

Nice-to-have later

remove pack

doctor

community packs

anchored file updates

pack marketplace

pack version migrations

That gives you a focused, powerful first release.

22. The real product vision

The big vision here is stronger than “CLI that writes files.”

Grove becomes:

a context-engineering operating system generator for software projects

It gives each repo:

a lean core

the right process artifacts

smart skills

structured handoffs

scoped rules

a manageable lifecycle

That is a very good product direction, Jeff.

The next best step is to turn this into a concrete engineering spec with:

exact Pydantic schemas

exact folder layout for packs

exact pack.yaml schema

exact manifest.toml schema

exact TUI screens and state transitions

first-pass CLI command signatures

I can write that next as a proper v1 technical design doc.
